MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Nov-23-2015 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
DEFENDANT REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is denied. When a defendant moves for summary judgment in a medical malpractice action relying on an expert declaration opining that the defendant's conduct fell within the community standard of care, the defendant is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff comes forward with conflicting expert evidence. (Murro v. The Regents of the University of California (1989) 215 Cal.App.3d 977, 985.) Here, Plaintiffs submitted an expert declaration by Dr. Daniel Miller, M.D. in opposition to Defendants' motion for summary judgment. Dr. Miller opines that Defendant Lawrence Way, M.D. did not meet the standard of care when treating Plaintiff Katherine Weiner. (Declaration of Daniel Miller, M.D., paragraph 7.) Defendants' reliance upon St. Mary's Medical Center v. Superior Court is unpersuasive. The defendant in St. Mary's showed that the plaintiff's expert's opinion lacked foundation because the expert had misunderstood a key, objective fact: which doctors were in the operating room and which were not. (See St. Mary's Medical Center v. Superior Court (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1531.) In the case at hand, by contrast, Defendants complain that Plaintiffs' expert, Dr. Miller, did not accept Dr. Way's opinion testimony that the surgical procedure Dr. Way performed was necessary under the circumstances and within the standard of care. But this does not mean that Dr. Miller's opinion lacks foundation. Defendants have not met their burden to show "a legitimate question regarding the foundation of [Dr. Miller's] opinion." Id. at 1540. Plaintiff raised a triable issue of material fact as to whether Dr. Way met the relevant standard of care. Therefore, Defendants are not entitled to summary judgment. Order signed. Judge: Charles Crompton; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489
